title: Why Writing My Book Wasn't the Scary Part: A Lesson in Vulnerability
excerpt: In this raw solo episode, Aimee Rickabus reveals why sharing her book with the world was scarier than writing it. She opens up about fear, vulnerability, and the quiet strength it takes to honor invisible labor while stepping into your power.
metaDescription: CEO Aimee Rickabus shares why vulnerability, not writing, was her biggest fear. Learn to recognize fear as growth and honor invisible labor.
keyTakeaways: Fear is often a signal that you're growing and moving in the right direction.
Writing something vulnerable isn't as scary as sharing it with the world.
Women hold entire worlds together before 9 AM and still question if they've done enough.
Leading with vulnerability and beginner's mind creates authentic connection.
Invisible labor of women deserves recognition and honor in leadership conversations.
Taking the leap requires moving forward even when your hands are shaking.
Perseverance through burnout and fear is part of the journey to authentic leadership.

pullQuotes.text: There are women holding entire worlds together before 9 AM, and still questioning if they've done enough.

content:
There's something profoundly vulnerable about putting your truth out into the world. As I sit here reflecting on the journey of launching The Manage Her, I'm struck by a realization that might surprise you: writing the book wasn't the scary part. The terrifying part was hitting publish and letting the world see what I had created.

In this special solo episode, I wanted to pull back the curtain and share what it really feels like to stand on the edge of your comfort zone and leap into something vulnerable, honest, and completely public. Because if you're anything like me, you might think that the act of creation is the hardest part. But what I've learned is that creation is just the beginning.

The Quiet Terror of Going Public

When I was deep in the writing process, pouring my experiences as a CEO and mother of six onto the page, I felt safe in my creative bubble. The words flowed, the insights crystallized, and I was in my element. But the moment I knew this work would leave my hands and enter the world, everything changed. Suddenly, I wasn't just a woman processing her experiences privately—I was about to become someone who had opinions about invisible labor, women's leadership, and the impossible standards we navigate daily.

The fear wasn't about whether the book was good enough. It was about whether I was ready to be seen as someone who had something important to say about these topics that matter so deeply to me. It was about stepping into a level of visibility that felt both necessary and terrifying.

This experience taught me something crucial about fear that I want to share with you: fear is often our internal compass pointing toward growth. When your hands are shaking and your heart is racing, it's usually because you're about to do something that matters. You're about to expand beyond who you've been into who you're becoming.

The Weight of Invisible Labor

One of the core themes that emerged as I prepared to share this work was the recognition of just how much invisible labor women carry. There are women holding entire worlds together before nine AM, orchestrating the complex dance of family logistics, emotional support, and professional demands, and still questioning if they've done enough by the end of the day.

This isn't just about the practical tasks, though those are significant. It's about the mental load, the emotional labor, the constant scanning for what needs attention, what needs fixing, what needs nurturing. It's about being the chief operating officer of multiple domains simultaneously while rarely receiving recognition for the expertise this requires.

As I prepared for speaking engagements, including an upcoming opportunity at Stanford, I kept returning to this theme because it's so fundamental to everything we experience as women in leadership. We're not just managing our professional responsibilities—we're managing entire ecosystems of care, connection, and coordination.

Vulnerability as a Leadership Strength

What I've discovered in this process is that leading with vulnerability isn't weakness—it's strategic authenticity. When I approach new challenges with what I call a beginner's mind, acknowledging that I don't have all the answers but I'm committed to learning and growing, something magical happens. It creates space for real connection and genuine impact.

This approach has been essential as I've navigated the intersection of motherhood and high-level leadership. There's no playbook for being the CEO of a nine-figure technology company while raising six children. There's no manual for writing a book about women's leadership while actively living the complexities you're writing about.

What there is, instead, is the opportunity to lead by example—to show other women that you can honor your multiple identities, acknowledge your fears, and still step boldly into your power. You can admit that you're figuring it out as you go while still being someone others can look to for guidance and inspiration.

The Courage to Take Your Leap

Every woman has a leap waiting for her. Maybe it's starting that business you've been dreaming about. Maybe it's having the difficult conversation that could transform your relationship. Maybe it's setting the boundary you've been afraid to set or pursuing the opportunity that feels simultaneously perfect and terrifying.

Whatever your leap is, I want you to know that the fear you're feeling is not a stop sign—it's a green light. It's your internal wisdom telling you that this matters enough to be scared about. It's your growth edge calling you forward into the next version of yourself.

The key is learning to move forward even when your hands are shaking. Courage isn't the absence of fear; it's the decision to act in alignment with your values and vision despite the fear. It's recognizing that the discomfort you feel in your stretch zone is actually the sensation of expansion.

Honoring Your Journey

As I reflect on this journey from conception to publication to sharing my work with the world, I'm struck by how much of our growth happens in the spaces between the big moments. It happens in the daily choice to show up authentically. It happens in the decision to honor both your ambitions and your humanity. It happens in the quiet moments when you choose to trust your voice and your vision.

If you're in a season of launching something new, navigating fear, or simply trying to find your spark again after a period of burnout or uncertainty, I want you to remember this: you are not alone. The challenges you're facing, the doubts you're wrestling with, the dreams you're nurturing—they're all part of a larger story of women refusing to diminish themselves to make others comfortable.

You have something valuable to contribute to this world. Your perspective matters. Your experience has equipped you with wisdom that someone else needs to hear. And while it might feel scary to step into that truth, the world needs what you have to offer.

The leap is worth it. You are worth it. And the women who will benefit from your courage to be visible, vulnerable, and authentic are waiting for you to take that first brave step forward.

quiz.title: What's Your Relationship with Vulnerability?
quiz.description: Inspired by episode 17 of The Manage Her® Podcast, discover how you approach the scary but necessary act of showing up authentically in your leadership and life.

questions.question: When you're about to share something important about yourself, what goes through your mind?

options.text: I need to make sure I've thought this through completely before I say anything
Here goes nothing—my heart is racing but I'm going for it
I'll share through my actions rather than making a big announcement
This feels scary, which probably means it's exactly what I need to do

questions.question: How do you typically handle moments when you feel like you're not doing 'enough' despite managing countless responsibilities?

options.text: I make lists and analyze what I could optimize or do better next time
I reach out to friends or family and talk through what I'm feeling
I remind myself that I don't need recognition—I know what I've accomplished
I sit with the feeling and try to understand what it's teaching me about my expectations

questions.question: When you're facing a new challenge or opportunity that feels beyond your current comfort zone, you:

options.text: Research extensively and create a plan before taking any steps forward
Feel the fear but jump in anyway, figuring you'll learn as you go
Focus on what you can control and take practical steps without overthinking it
See it as a chance to discover something new about yourself, even if it's uncomfortable

questions.question: What does 'authentic leadership' look like to you in practice?

options.text: Being thoughtful about when and how you share your experiences to help others
Showing up as your full self, imperfections and all, so others feel permission to do the same
Leading by example through consistent actions rather than grand speeches
Being willing to admit you don't have all the answers and learning alongside your team

questions.question: When you think about all the 'invisible labor' you do—the planning, organizing, anticipating, and managing that often goes unnoticed—how does it make you feel?

options.text: I wish others understood how much thought and preparation goes into keeping everything running smoothly
I want to talk about it more openly so other women know they're not alone in this experience
It's just part of life—I don't need a medal for doing what needs to be done
It makes me curious about why I take on so much and what I might learn from examining these patterns

questions.question: Your hands are metaphorically shaking as you're about to take a big leap. What helps you move forward?

options.text: Reminding myself that I've prepared as much as I can, and now it's time to trust the process
Remembering that courage isn't the absence of fear—it's feeling afraid and doing it anyway
Focusing on the next concrete step rather than the enormity of the whole journey
Recognizing that the shaking means I'm about to grow into someone new

results.title: The Careful Curator
results.description: You're thoughtful about what you share and when, preferring to perfect your thoughts before putting them out into the world. This isn't weakness—it's wisdom. You understand that vulnerability requires intention, not impulse. Your challenge is recognizing when your desire for perfection might be keeping you from the very connections and growth you're seeking. This episode will resonate deeply with you as Aimee shares how the creation process felt safe, but hitting publish was terrifying—and why taking that leap anyway changed everything.

results.title: The Brave Heart
results.description: You lead with your heart on your sleeve and aren't afraid to share your truth, even when it feels uncomfortable. Your willingness to be open creates incredible connections with others, and people are drawn to your authenticity. Sometimes though, you might wonder if you're sharing too much too quickly, or if others think you're 'too much.' This episode will remind you that your vulnerability is actually your superpower, and help you see how leading with beginner's mind and openness creates the authentic leadership the world desperately needs.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You prefer to let your actions speak louder than your words, building trust through consistency rather than grand gestures of openness. You're the reliable force others count on, and you get things done without needing fanfare or recognition. Your challenge is that your incredible contributions—especially all that invisible labor—often go unnoticed, even by yourself. This episode will help you recognize the profound leadership you're already demonstrating and give you permission to step into more visible roles where your steady wisdom can have even greater impact.

results.title: The Truth Seeker
results.description: You're on a constant journey of self-discovery and growth, always asking the deeper questions about who you are and who you're becoming. You see vulnerability not as weakness but as a path to understanding, both for yourself and others. Your gift is helping others feel safe to explore their own truth through your example of honest self-reflection. This episode will speak directly to your soul as Aimee explores how fear often signals we're growing in the right direction, and why perseverance through discomfort is part of the journey to authentic leadership.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone, it's Aimee Rickabus and welcome back to The Manager. Today's episode is different. It's just me. No guests, no questions, just my mic, my heart, and a story I need to share. We're diving into something I'm living in real time. Perseverance. Not the loud, gritty kind, but the quiet courage it takes to stand by the edge of your comfort zone with your heart pounding and leap anyway. Terrified, but trusting. This is about what happens when you share your truth with the world. And trust me, it's scarier than writing a book.

Writing a book wasn't the hardest part. And people keep asking me about writing The Manager. They ask me if it was hard. And honestly, it wasn't hard. The words poured out. I've lived this life. I've led teams and built businesses, raising kids, wrestled with my identity and my purpose, and the story was already in me. The hard part actually is sharing it, releasing it to the world, letting people read it, judge it, see me. This is where the fear really rushed in for me.

It's funny because for the last two weeks, I've been on and off sick, which is so interesting because I haven't been sick in 10 years. Honestly, it's really strange. And I think it's just the part of me that's really been struggling with being so honest and being so transparent and being so visible. Sharing your authentic self with the world is a very vulnerable thing to do.

I think all artists feel it. I remember this feeling back in film school. I would write a screenplay and it was easy to write a screenplay. It's not easy to have someone else read your screenplay. It's easy to make a short film. It's hard to let other people watch your short films. It's the reckoning. It's the other side of creativity, the part where we share it with other people. That sometimes can be so scary, but it's also that's where the legacy is. That's where we make such a big difference.

We have to dig in and push past the pain point and push past the fear point in life. And that's what this episode is about. It's about what happens when we push past that fear. When we stop waiting to feel ready and we just choose to leap anyway because that's where transformation begins.

I would have loved to wait. I don't feel ready. Honestly, I woke up this morning. I launched my book this morning and I just rolled out of bed like a regular girl and had no idea how today was going to go. But I'm a builder. I started my first business at 22. I know how to hustle. I know grit. But I'm sharing something this raw, this personal, this book that took me somewhere new. And the podcast, too, honestly, being really real with you guys, being here, being this voice, sharing my thoughts, my private thoughts with all y'all. I mean, it's definitely out of my comfort zone.

And comfort zones aren't just about avoiding hard work. Sometimes they're the places where we feel safe and we feel in control. And stepping out of it isn't just a catchy quote. It's soul deep work. It's trusting yourself when every shadow, fear of judgment, fear of failure, being too much or not being enough. It all tries to pull you back. Those are the moments when you have to dig in really deep and just trust the process. That's when you have to just have a plan, a strategic plan, and you just go with a plan. Don't listen to your mind that's trying to hold you back and you just do what you need to do.

It's honestly, it really is jumping off that 30-foot cliff in Maui. And the longer you stand on the ledge and look down, the harder it's going to be. So sometimes you just got to jump. When it's time to jump, you jump.

Launching The Manager brought all of those shadows to the surface. But I've learned this fear is just a signpost. It's proof that you're about to grow. A lot of times it's the thing that's trying to hold you back from your own personal growth, too. So sometimes you have to see your fear and go, "Okay, I see you fear. Are you trying to hold me back from my personal growth right now?" And you use it, you use that fear to show you where the scared points are. Where is the edge? And once you know where the edge is, you can go beyond that edge, right?

And I try to tell my kids all the time don't be scared. Use that fear to show you where the edge is and then you can go past it. Once you push past your fear, it disappears. Fear is only there as long as you allow it to be there, right? So, as long as you push past your fear and you do whatever you're afraid of doing, poof, the fear evaporates.

So, I tried to approach this especially today with just this beginner's mind and letting go. In yoga, there's this concept called beginner's mind, which I love. I learned this stuff when I was about 30 years old and I needed it so bad and I talk about it in my book. It's when you show up open, with an open heart, no ego, and no ideas of what you're supposed to know, and you're just ready to learn. And that's how I'm approaching this launch. This is so outside my wheelhouse. So, let's just have a beginner's mind.

I don't want to come in as a seasoned CEO with all the answers, but I just want to come in as a woman with a story. And I'm okay doing it scared. And I'm okay doing it without knowing exactly what I'm doing and doing it perfect. Because perfect doesn't matter. Perfect is this construct that holds us back from our growth, from our potential. It's let go of perfect and let's just do it. Let's do things and be scared. Let's do things and be afraid, but push past it and then it will be gone. The fear will be gone.

There's no playbook for vulnerability, no spreadsheet for sharing your heart. So, I've adopted the mantra, let it go, let it happen. That doesn't mean I'm not strategic. In fact, strategy is awesome. It means I'm releasing the outcome. I'm focusing on the offering. Here it is. I'm putting it out into the world. I hope that this is a part of my legacy for my children, my daughters, my grandchildren. And I hope that this book is something that people will look at and get value from my truth, my work, and trusting it will land where it's meant to.

I'm sharing this because so many of you are standing at your own edge. Maybe it's a business you're scared to start, a job you need to leave, or a truth you're afraid to speak to your partner, to your boss, to yourself. That moment when your hands shake and your heart races, it's not a sign that you're not ready. It's a sign that you're exactly where you need to be in that exact moment. Because that's where life blooms. That's where you become the woman you're meant to be. When you confront your fears, when you face your fears, when you speak the words you're afraid to speak. This is how we become. We must become. We must evolve. We must grow. And that means we have to persevere.

Perseverance is often quiet. We think of perseverance as loud, bold moves, and sweaty breakthroughs. But sometimes it's just quiet. It's sending the email. It's publishing the post. It's walking into your book launch with a brave face, even when your inner critic is screaming. If you're in that quiet fight, pushing through your fear, I see you. I'm with you. This community is with you.

Before we wrap up, I want to thank the people who've held me through this journey. To the Seaside Sisterhood, my incredible neighbors. You guys are awesome. Seriously, I couldn't have gotten through today without your group thread text. Everyone sharing, I just bought your book and here's a picture of the receipt and the love that I'm getting from my sisters at office hours. Your encouragement and the belief in me. You've helped me carry this book through. This book wasn't just mine. It was lifted, loved, and launched by all of you. From the bottom of my heart, thank you.

One more thing, I'm thrilled to share that on May 10th, I'll be speaking at Stanford University alongside Baja founder Deja Foxx. It's an honor to join a conversation that bridges business, public health, and advocacy. Recently, we've seen real progress in protecting our families, the removal of artificial food dyes. The toxic rainbow from our food supply. Dyes like red 40, yellow five, and blue one linked to hyperactivity, allergies, and worse are finally being addressed. As a mom and an advocate for wellness, I'm grateful to every voice, policy makers, citizens, parents who made this happen. Change starts with us, step by step.

I've been in the fight with the moms. Thank you, Zen Honeycutt, for all your hard work at Moms Across America. I see you, girl. I see your hustle. I'd love to have you on the show. Shout out to you big time. There have been some serious moms in this game for a long time trying to get these changes made. And I see you. I see your hard work. I see how you were out there trying to protect not only your own children, but all of the children. And I appreciate all of the work you've done.

In The Manager, I write about women as the CEOs of their households, managing everything, calendars, meals, emotions, traditions, without a title or a paycheck. It's project management, emotional leadership, and logistics, all in the margins of your day. If you're a mom, I see you. I see your late nights, your endless lists, your heart holding it all together. One of my favorite lines from the book is, "There are women holding entire worlds together before 9:00 a.m. and still questioning if they've done enough." Take up space, rest, celebrate yourself the way you are and the way you celebrate everyone else. And if you're not a mom, but you've been the glue for others, this is you, too. Your leadership, your love, it matters. It's time we honor it.

Take the leap. Whatever launching the book looks like for you, do it. Do it scared. Do it imperfectly, but do it. Because on the other side of fear isn't just growth, it's freedom.

Thank you for being here. If this resonated, share it with a friend. If you're in the thick of your own leap, I'd love to hear about it. DM me, tag me, or just write it down for yourself. I'm Aimee Rickabus, and this is The Manager. Until next time.
